title: Frontier ML roundup for 2026-05-20
research_dossier_markdown:
## Episode Metadata

Episode ID: episode-2026-05-20-01-frontier-ml-roundup

Title: Frontier ML roundup for 2026-05-20

Episode type: frontier_ml_roundup

Job ID: episode-2026-05-20-01-frontier-ml-roundup-dossier

NotebookLM target: Deep Dive, Long length. This dossier is factual source material, not a performed script.

Papers covered: arxiv-2605.20164, arxiv-2605.20167, arxiv-2605.20170, arxiv-2605.20172, arxiv-2605.20173, arxiv-2605.20174, arxiv-2605.20176, arxiv-2605.20177, arxiv-2605.20179, arxiv-2605.20182.

## Why These Papers Are Grouped

This roundup is a cross-section of frontier ML-adjacent work where the core question is not just model capability, but the surrounding system that turns capability into reliable behavior. Several papers ask how to feed models better evidence or structure: ClinSeekAgent gives clinical agents raw EHR, web, and imaging tools; KoRe compresses knowledge graphs into discrete graph tokens; AUDITS builds a manipulation-localization benchmark that stresses detector generalization; the staged VLM post-training paper separates visual perception from reasoning. Others focus on control and runtime design: POW3R dynamically reweights rubric rewards during GRPO, TIDE schedules MoE diffusion-model experts across denoising steps, and the stochastic-deterministic-boundary paper frames production agents around verifiable commits.

The set also includes two useful contrast cases outside the usual foundation-model lane: HaorFloodAlert shows how retrospective ML for disaster warning can look strong while carrying operational leakage and deployment risks, and the ASP grid-planning paper shows a symbolic constraint-native alternative to learning-heavy infrastructure planning. The EEG microstate tokenizer paper rounds out the episode as a representation-learning claim whose ambition exceeds its current evidence.

## Concise Thesis

The common lesson is that frontier ML progress is increasingly bottlenecked by evidence acquisition, representation boundaries, and system-level control. The strongest papers make one of those bottlenecks measurable: criteria with no rollout variance cannot train GRPO; clinical agents can improve when allowed to search raw evidence, but can also drown in irrelevant information; image-forgery detectors that look competent in-distribution break under source, generator, and manipulation-size shifts; VLMs may need staged perceptual grounding before reasoning training; diffusion MoE inference can exploit stable expert routing.

The skeptical through-line is that many claims lean on experimental setups that are too private, too synthetic, too judge-mediated, too benchmark-specific, or too operationally incomplete. The episode should reward papers that expose a real bottleneck while keeping pressure on the evidence: Was the model actually better, or did it get more information? Was the representation universal, or just useful under a weak split? Was the benchmark testing real-world robustness, or the construction pipeline? Was the system validated operationally, or only retrospectively?

## Per-Paper Claim Versus Evidence

### arxiv-2605.20164: POW3R Dynamic Rubric Rewards

Claim: POW3R dynamically reweights prompt-specific rubric criteria by rollout-level contrast so GRPO trains on criteria that currently distinguish policy outputs while preserving the human rubric target.

Evidence: The authors compare base models, binary all-or-nothing rewards, static weighted rubric rewards, category-balanced rubric rewards, and POW3R under GRPO on an in-house 10k multimodal rubric dataset and HealthBench English. They use three base policies per setting and LLM judges for per-criterion reward and held-out evaluation.

Best reading: This is a clean methods paper around a real GRPO failure mode. If all rollouts pass or fail a criterion, standardized group-relative advantage has no useful signal from that criterion. POW3R's bounded, smoothed, within-category reweighting is a plausible fix.

Skeptical reading: The evidence is heavily judge-mediated and dataset-internal. Training rewards, adaptive weights, and held-out scores all depend on related GPT-5.4-family rubric judges. The main multimodal dataset is private, and reported gains lack confidence intervals or significance tests.

Paper Radio angle: Useful as a reward-shaping segment, not a settled general result. The critique should focus on whether POW3R is a robust algorithmic improvement or a tuned response to one judge/dataset regime.

### arxiv-2605.20167: HaorFloodAlert

Claim: A deseasonalized RF+XGBoost ensemble using Sentinel-1 SAR, weather, and upstream Barak proxies can forecast Sunamganj haor flood risk 72 hours ahead with 89.6% LOOCV accuracy on 77 real-SAR events.

Evidence: The authors evaluate 77 post-2014 Sentinel-1 events, including 32 floods, and use 54 pre-2014 proxy events for training and stability checks. They report LOOCV metrics, compare raw temperature to climatological temperature anomaly, validate Otsu SAR flood mapping on three events, and add feature ablations and alert/crop-damage layers.

Best reading: The paper is unusually self-aware for an applied ML-for-disaster pilot. It explicitly detects a seasonal temperature shortcut, replaces raw temperature with a monthly anomaly, reports the real-event confusion matrix, rejects an overfit LSTM result, and flags proxy circularity and operational lag.

Skeptical reading: The headline rests on a very small evaluation set, augmentation inside folds, a hand-selected operational threshold, architecture choices influenced by the full real+proxy setup, synthetic proxy SAR features, and unvalidated discharge/trend and crop-loss layers. McNemar's p=0.724 against logistic regression weakens any strong ensemble-superiority claim.

Paper Radio angle: Strong operational cautionary tale. Treat it as a promising pilot, not a validated flood-warning system.

### arxiv-2605.20170: KoRe Graph Tokens

Claim: KoRe encodes 1-hop Wikidata star graphs with a GNN plus directional residual vector quantization into 20 discrete knowledge tokens, then injects them into Qwen3-8B to match or beat textual KG prompting with fewer tokens.

Evidence: KoRe-base is trained on Tri-REx Star synthetic factual sentences, KoRe-QA is further fine-tuned on SimpleQuestions, and evaluation uses Hit@1/3/5/10 over answer object tokens on Tri-REx, SimpleQuestions, and zero-shot WebQSP. Baselines include vanilla Qwen3-8B, textualized triples, LoRA-only, and selected ConceptFormer numbers.

Best reading: The paper tackles a real context-cost bottleneck in KG-augmented LLMs and gives concrete evidence that compact graph-token representations can recover much of the value of textual triples.

Skeptical reading: The central metric is teacher-forced token ranking, not generated-answer exact match, F1, retrieval QA, or hallucination-grounding measurement. Graph extraction is assumed available, and the comparison to older ConceptFormer results is not apples-to-apples.

Paper Radio angle: Promising discrete graph-prefix idea, but not proof that it grounds LLMs better than strong textual, retrieval, or continuous-prefix baselines.

### arxiv-2605.20172: ASP Distribution-Grid Planning

Claim: Long-term distribution-grid evolution can be formulated as an Answer Set Programming planning/reconfiguration problem that preserves radiality, redundancy, degree constraints, and target-line irreversibility while finding sequential or parallel intervention plans.

Evidence: The authors encode the problem in CLINGO and evaluate bounded versus exponential horizon search and sequential versus parallel plans on 175 synthetic graph-pair instances plus 7 anonymized French urban grid subnetworks, under 1800 CPU-second and 2 GB limits.

Best reading: The formalization is clean and domain-grounded. It translates DSO rules into graph constraints, defines add/remove/switch actions, and externally validates intermediate states against those rules.

Skeptical reading: This is a narrow topological abstraction, not full grid planning. It omits power-flow feasibility, capacity, budgets, construction timing, demand uncertainty, and costed investment scheduling. It has no comparison to MILP, CP-SAT, classical planning, domain heuristics, or human plans.

Paper Radio angle: Useful contrast against ML-heavy infrastructure claims. It shows the value of symbolic constraints, while also showing how far a clean abstraction can be from operational planning.

### arxiv-2605.20173: Stochastic-Deterministic Boundary For Agents

Claim: Production LLM agents should be designed around a named stochastic-deterministic boundary, then selected from six runtime architecture patterns using a five-step workload methodology.

Evidence: The paper reports an audit of five open-source agent frameworks, classifies 21 published agent failure post-mortems, applies the methodology to five constructed workloads, and provides one runnable contract-renewal reference implementation using a projected IBM Telco Customer Churn dataset.

Best reading: The stochastic-deterministic boundary is an actionable engineering abstraction. Proposer, verifier, commit, and reject signals map cleanly onto real LLM-to-action systems and connect agent design to distributed-systems patterns such as logs, sagas, state machines, supervision, and human approval.

Skeptical reading: The evidence is mostly illustrative, author-coded, or constructed. The audit and post-mortem counts are under-specified, only one workload is implemented, and no reliability gains are measured against existing architecture-decision practices or deployed systems.

Paper Radio angle: Cover as a sharp architecture essay with useful vocabulary, not as validated ML research.

### arxiv-2605.20174: AUDITS Manipulation Localization Benchmark

Claim: AUDITS is a 530K-image benchmark for image manipulation localization that lets researchers stress-test detectors across image source, manipulation type, manipulation size, and human-perceived edit quality.

Evidence: The dataset uses VisualNews and COCO, diffusion-based replacement/removal/insertion edits from 11 techniques, masks, and pristine images. The paper evaluates PSCC-Net, MMFusion, HiFi, EVP, TruFor, SIDA, DOLOS, UperNet, and domain-generalization variants across in-domain and cross-domain splits, size-conditioned results, transfer to MagicBrush and CocoGLIDE, and a small AMT quality analysis.

Best reading: This is a genuinely useful benchmark paper. It separates source shift from manipulation-type shift, includes modern diffusion/inpainting generators, evaluates many detectors under one protocol, and exposes brittle generalization hidden by single-dataset reporting.

Skeptical reading: The dataset is still synthetic and mask-defined. VisualNews masks depend on ODISE rather than human ground truth, the ambiguous-pixel evaluation uses empirical thresholds, and the human-quality study covers only a small subset of the dataset. The benchmark may measure robustness to this construction pipeline as much as real-world manipulation robustness.

Paper Radio angle: One of the better anchors for the episode because it converts a broad concern, forensic robustness under modern generators, into a multi-axis benchmark with public data.

### arxiv-2605.20176: ClinSeekAgent

Claim: Clinical agents do better when they actively retrieve EHR, web, and imaging evidence instead of answering from pre-curated patient context, and teacher trajectories can distill that behavior into an open model.

Evidence: ClinSeek-Bench pairs curated-input versions of existing EHR and multimodal clinical tasks with an automated evidence-seeking setting over raw MIMIC-derived EHR/CXR data. The paper evaluates 12 models on 1,800 text-only and 989 multimodal examples, then fine-tunes Qwen3.5-35B-A3B on 7,204 Claude Opus 4.6-generated trajectories and evaluates on AgentEHR-Bench.

Best reading: The paired benchmark design is the paper's strongest idea. It asks whether the system can acquire needed evidence, not only answer from an oracle context. The paper also reports task-family failures, confidence intervals, tool inventory, inference budgets, and a training-time transfer experiment.

Skeptical reading: The automated evidence-seeking condition often has a broader evidence surface than curated input: full raw history before cutoff, external browser search, image classifiers/report generators, and benchmark vocabulary lookup. Gains may reflect added information and specialized tools rather than better clinical reasoning or agentic planning. Decision-making tasks degrade broadly, and some multimodal mortality tasks show large negative deltas.

Paper Radio angle: High-value episode material. It directly extends the evidence-acquisition framing: models may recognize evidence when handed it, but the harder system question is whether they can decide what to retrieve, inspect, and trust.

### arxiv-2605.20177: Capability-Staged VLM Post-Training

Claim: Staging VLM post-training as visual perception, textual reasoning, then visual reasoning improves accuracy and shortens reasoning traces compared with merged training.

Evidence: The authors synthesize perception-focused QA from DOCCI captions, train Qwen2.5-VL-7B and Qwen3-VL-8B with GRPO/RLVR across three stages, and compare staged training against merged training, alternative stage orders, SFT for perception, difficulty curricula, and open-weight VLM baselines on visual math and perception benchmarks.

Best reading: The ablation stack is coherent. The paper directly tests perception+reasoning versus reasoning-only, staged versus merged, order swaps, RLVR versus SFT, and capability plus difficulty curricula. Staged training wins fairly consistently across main and appendix tables.

Skeptical reading: The causal claim that perception is the bottleneck rests on imperfect proxies: caption-derived labels, caption-vs-image filtering, and Claude-Haiku perception-error judgments with limited manual checking. The headline staged-vs-merged setup is partly confounded by visual-encoder training schedules, though appendix variants reduce that concern.

Paper Radio angle: Good hook: longer thinking cannot fix bad seeing. Present it as a promising curriculum recipe, not definitive proof that visual perception is the dominant VLM reasoning bottleneck.

### arxiv-2605.20179: TIDE MoE Diffusion LLM Inference

Claim: MoE diffusion LLM inference can be accelerated losslessly by exploiting stable expert routing across nearby denoising steps and refreshing GPU-resident experts only at optimized intervals.

Evidence: TIDE is evaluated on LLaDA2.0-mini and LLaDA2.0-flash using sanitized MBPP, compared against Fiddler-style CPU expert execution and Mixtral-Offload-style per-step expert swapping under several GPU expert budgets, block sizes, output lengths, and confidence thresholds.

Best reading: The systems observation is plausible and useful: MoE diffusion LLM routing is temporally stable across nearby denoising steps, so expert migration can be amortized instead of repeated every step.

Skeptical reading: The evaluation is narrow: one benchmark, two model variants, two GPU classes, throughput-only reporting, and very low absolute token/s numbers. The word lossless is algorithmically plausible if placement changes do not change router decisions, but systems-level execution still depends on CPU/GPU numerical equivalence and synchronization behavior.

Paper Radio angle: Focused systems segment. Treat TIDE as a promising scheduling heuristic for a specific emerging model class, not a general free lunch for serving.

### arxiv-2605.20182: EEG Microstate Tokenizer

Claim: A k-means EEG microstate tokenizer trained on six-channel sleep EEG can act as a universal discrete representation that beats raw and STFT features on sleep staging, SEED emotion recognition, and motor imagery classification.

Evidence: The authors fit a 1000-cluster streaming k-means tokenizer on GFP peaks from six HSP sleep EEG channels, then feed token sequences through task-specific deep models on HSP sleep staging, SEED emotion recognition, and PhysioNet motor movement/imagery. They compare against raw EEG and STFT band-power representations with accuracy and Cohen's kappa.

Best reading: The representation question is concrete, and the same tokenizer is tested across three different EEG tasks. Main tables report gains over raw and STFT baselines in sleep staging, emotion recognition, and motor imagery.

Skeptical reading: The universality claim is much stronger than the evidence. The tokenizer is trained and evaluated on HSP for sleep staging, subject-held-out splits are unclear, strong modern EEG/self-supervised baselines are missing, and architecture changes confound representation comparisons. Motor imagery remains weak in absolute terms.

Paper Radio angle: Provocative early empirical probe. Useful if framed as a biologically motivated tokenization idea, not a demonstrated universal EEG representation.

## Strongest Contributions

1. AUDITS provides the most useful public infrastructure contribution. Its value is not a new detector but a stress test across source, generator, manipulation type, manipulation size, and external datasets.

2. ClinSeekAgent has the strongest systems-evaluation idea: compare curated evidence against raw evidence-seeking for the same task labels. Even with confounds, that design asks a better question than passive clinical QA alone.

3. The staged VLM post-training paper has the strongest training-method ablation package. It gives a plausible recipe for separating perception, text reasoning, and visual reasoning rather than mixing them all into one post-training soup.

4. POW3R has the cleanest mechanism. It identifies a specific mathematical dead zone in group-relative reward learning and proposes a conservative dynamic rubric weighting rule.

5. TIDE has the sharpest systems observation. Stable expert routing across denoising steps is a concrete property that can be turned into an inference scheduler.

6. KoRe makes the graph-conditioning bottleneck tangible by trading textual triples for a small number of learned discrete graph tokens.

7. The stochastic-deterministic-boundary paper contributes strong practitioner vocabulary for production agents, especially around verifier/commit/reject interfaces.

8. HaorFloodAlert is valuable because its limitations are as instructive as its results: seasonal leakage, synthetic proxy history, tiny real-event counts, SAR latency, and deployment gaps.

9. The ASP grid paper is a useful reminder that some infrastructure problems are naturally constraint-native and should not be forced into ML framing.

10. The EEG tokenizer paper is interesting as a representation idea, but its evidence is the least mature relative to the breadth of its claims.

## Serious Weaknesses And Red Flags

The largest shared red flag is evidence inflation: several papers report attractive numbers under conditions that may not isolate the claimed mechanism.

ClinSeekAgent's automated evidence-seeking condition often gets more information than curated input, including raw longitudinal history, browser search, imaging tools, and benchmark vocabulary lookup. The right question is not simply whether agent mode wins, but whether it wins after controlling for evidence surface, tool power, and inference budget.

HaorFloodAlert's 89.6% LOOCV accuracy is based on only 77 real-SAR events and 32 floods. The paper is careful about limitations, but the headline can easily be overread as operational validation. It is not.

POW3R's main result depends on private in-house data and related LLM judges for training, weighting, and evaluation. Without human-scored validation, uncertainty estimates, and judge-noise ablations, the paper is promising rather than conclusive.

KoRe's key metric is answer-token ranking, not open answer generation or end-to-end retrieval QA. That makes the graph-token claim narrower than the grounding language suggests.

The staged VLM paper uses generated perception labels and model-mediated perception-error diagnosis. The method may work, but the bottleneck diagnosis should be treated as supported, not proven.

AUDITS is large and useful, but synthetic. Its VisualNews labels inherit ODISE mask noise, and its ambiguous-pixel metric introduces nonstandard thresholds that can shape conclusions.

TIDE's evaluation is too narrow for broad serving claims: sanitized MBPP, two LLaDA2.0 variants, two GPU classes, throughput-only analysis, and low absolute throughput.

The agent-architecture paper uses strong language for concepts validated mostly through author-coded audits, constructed workloads, and one toy-like reference implementation.

The ASP grid paper should not be presented as ML. It is a symbolic planning paper with a simplified graph abstraction and missing operational constraints.

The EEG tokenizer paper's universal-representation framing is overbroad given unclear subject splits, same-corpus sleep tokenizer/evaluation, weak baselines, and architecture confounds.

## Missing Baselines And Ablations

For POW3R, the important missing comparisons are multi-reward/objective-decoupled GRPO, variance-only dynamic weights, curriculum rubric weighting, uniform-per-criterion/category rewards, judge-noise robustness, rollout group-size sensitivity, and hyperparameter sensitivity for alpha bounds, EMA, lambda, epsilon, and valid-verdict thresholds.

For HaorFloodAlert, the key missing baselines are operational weather/gauge rules, logistic regression or calibrated GAM with the same features, seasonal climatology, FFWC-style discharge rules, nested real-SAR-only tuning, NDWI missingness-aware imputation, and end-to-end validation of discharge/trend alert adjustments.

For KoRe, the missing controls are budget-matched textualization, retrieval-only KG answer selection, continuous graph-prefix conditioning, simpler triple encoders, prompt-compression/triple-selection baselines, same-backbone ConceptFormer-style implementation, token-count ablations, quantization ablations, and codebook utilization diagnostics.

For the ASP grid paper, the missing baselines are MILP, CP-SAT, SAT/classical planning, greedy/local-search heuristics, decomposition, and human expert plans. It also needs invariant-family ablations and objective-function comparisons.

For the stochastic-deterministic-boundary paper, the missing evidence is independent incident coding, comparisons to established architecture-decision methods, replayed real production incidents, stress tests under model-version changes, and ablations of verifier, durable commit, and typed reject signals.

For AUDITS, the important missing pieces are training comparisons against existing manipulation datasets under the same budget, artifact/difference heuristics, foundation-feature anomaly baselines, human localization baselines, sensitivity to tau and alpha in ambiguous-pixel scoring, manual checks of VisualNews masks, and size/source distribution equalization.

For ClinSeekAgent, the missing controls are retrieval-only raw-data baselines, curated-input plus identical imaging tools, no-browser conditions, same-window evidence conditions, cost/latency/tool-call-normalized comparisons, tool-family ablations, repeated stochastic runs, and clinician-adjudicated subsets.

For staged VLM post-training, the missing controls are merged training with the same visual-encoder schedule, random or repeated-stage curricula, perception-only intermediate evaluation, DOCCI QA without hard negative filtering, reward-component ablations, data-quality sensitivity, and training-seed variance.

For TIDE, the missing baselines are MoE-Infinity-style offloading, GPU-only upper bounds, fixed-interval grid-search refresh, static top-B expert cache, lookahead/prefetch scheduling, latency/energy/cost reporting, hit-rate and PCIe/NVLink traffic measurements, and routing/generalization tests across workloads and batch sizes.

For the EEG tokenizer, the missing baselines are modern EEG foundation/self-supervised models, standard task-specific EEG architectures, classical low-count microstate baselines, full-channel baselines, subject-independent split ablations, tokenizer-source ablations, non-microstate quantizers, vocabulary-size curves, and leakage checks.

## Comparison Axes

### Evidence Acquisition Versus Privileged Evidence

ClinSeekAgent is the central paper on this axis. It explicitly compares answering from curated patient context with actively retrieving raw EHR, web, and image evidence. The critique is that the active condition may also receive privileged or expanded information. The memory framing from recent episodes applies directly: a system should be judged by whether it can obtain the right evidence, not only use evidence after it is handed over.

The staged VLM paper is a related perceptual version. If visual reasoning improves only after perception-focused training, then the system was not bottlenecked by chain-of-thought length alone. It needed better access to visual facts.

KoRe also sits on this axis, but for structured knowledge. It asks whether graph facts should be injected as compact learned tokens rather than verbose textual triples.

### Benchmark Construction Versus Real-World Robustness

AUDITS is the strongest benchmark paper because it intentionally varies source, generator, manipulation type, and size. But its own construction choices, synthetic edits, segmentation masks, ambiguous pixels, and limited human quality labels remain part of what detectors may learn.

HaorFloodAlert is the applied version of the same issue. Retrospective SAR-labeled events and proxy history are not the same as live hydrological forecasting with sensor latency, cross-border river dynamics, and operational decision costs.

The EEG tokenizer also depends heavily on benchmark construction. Without subject-independent splits and stronger baselines, broad transfer claims remain fragile.

### Judge-Mediated Evaluation

POW3R, the staged VLM paper, and parts of ClinSeekAgent depend heavily on LLM judges or model-generated trajectories. That is not disqualifying, but it means gains may partially reflect judge-family preferences, generated-label artifacts, or benchmark-specific scoring affordances.

The key listener question: where would a human expert, independent verifier, or non-overlapping evaluation protocol change the result?

### Runtime Architecture And Control

TIDE and the stochastic-deterministic-boundary paper are both about runtime rather than raw model weights. TIDE optimizes where experts live during diffusion-model denoising. The SDB paper optimizes where stochastic model proposals end and deterministic system commitments begin.

POW3R also belongs here in training-time form: it controls which rubric criteria receive learning pressure as the policy changes.

### Symbolic Constraints Versus Learned Heuristics

The ASP grid paper is the cleanest symbolic contrast. It encodes hard grid constraints directly rather than learning them from data. That gives interpretability and rule satisfaction, but leaves out electrical and economic realism.

This comparison is useful against HaorFloodAlert and ClinSeekAgent: in high-stakes domains, learned predictions and agentic evidence gathering still need explicit constraints, operational validation, and decision boundaries.

## Verdict For The Listener

This roundup is strongest if framed around a question: what part of the system actually creates reliability?

For POW3R, reliability comes from keeping reward learning focused on rubric criteria that still distinguish rollouts. For ClinSeekAgent, it may come from active evidence access, but only if that access is controlled and clinically meaningful. For AUDITS, it comes from testing detectors across the shifts they will actually face. For staged VLM training, it may come from teaching models to see before asking them to reason. For TIDE, it comes from exploiting a stable runtime pattern rather than changing the model. For the agent-architecture paper, it comes from drawing a hard boundary between stochastic suggestions and deterministic commits.

The most important critical warning is that better numbers often come from a larger or cleaner evidence surface, a benchmark construction detail, a judge-mediated reward, or a simplified abstraction. These papers are most valuable when they reveal those dependencies rather than when they claim to have solved the domain.

Recommended coverage priority:

1. Lead with ClinSeekAgent because it directly embodies the evidence-acquisition theme and has high-stakes clinical implications with clear confounds.
2. Pair it with AUDITS as the benchmark counterpart: robust ML requires testing source, generator, size, and external-dataset shifts, not just in-distribution accuracy.
3. Use the staged VLM paper as the perception-before-reasoning training story.
4. Use POW3R and TIDE as compact mechanism papers: dynamic reward pressure and stable expert routing.
5. Use KoRe as the structured-knowledge representation segment.
6. Use the stochastic-deterministic-boundary paper as a practitioner architecture segment, clearly labeled as conceptual.
7. Use HaorFloodAlert as the applied cautionary tale about leakage, proxy data, and operational validation.
8. Use the ASP grid paper briefly as the symbolic-planning contrast case.
9. Use the EEG tokenizer last or as an optional segment because its claim-to-evidence gap is large and its connection to the frontier ML theme is weaker.

Overall verdict: cover the set as a systems-and-evidence roundup. The papers are less about one model becoming smarter in isolation and more about the scaffolding around models: what evidence they can access, what representation they receive, what reward signal they learn from, what runtime scheduler controls them, and what deterministic boundary prevents stochastic outputs from becoming unsafe actions.

## Source Notes And Local Input Paths

This dossier synthesizes the provided local review records. The review JSONs are compression/provenance inputs, not recommended NotebookLM upload sources.

Local review inputs:

- data/reviews/arxiv-2605.20164.json
- data/reviews/arxiv-2605.20167.json
- data/reviews/arxiv-2605.20170.json

- data/reviews/arxiv-2605.20179.json
- data/reviews/arxiv-2605.20182.json

Suggested original-paper upload budget should be used only for anchor papers where NotebookLM benefits from seeing details directly. For this episode, ClinSeekAgent and AUDITS are the best candidates: ClinSeekAgent anchors the active-evidence-acquisition theme and has nuanced task-family deltas, tool inventories, and confounds; AUDITS anchors the benchmark-shift theme and has many protocol details, tables, and metric choices that are hard to fully compress.

recommended_upload_sources.rationale: ClinSeekAgent is the episode's strongest anchor for active evidence acquisition. NotebookLM would benefit from the original tables and methods because the critique depends on task-family deltas, tool access, inference budgets, and curated-versus-raw evidence controls.

AUDITS is the benchmark anchor. The original paper is worth upload budget because its value and critique depend on protocol details: source/manipulation splits, detector tables, ambiguous-pixel scoring, human-quality labels, and external transfer results.
